lenses.guidance: v 2's 'glory and beauty' — Hebrew kavod ve-tiph'eret. Kavod is presence-weight; tiph'eret is splendour. The original audience hears that priestly clothing carries divine weight, not merely human pageantry.

Aaron carries the twelve tribes' names on his shoulders and over his heart (vv 9-12, 29) — the high priest physically bears Israel into God's presence. The pattern prefigures Christ as great high priest (Heb 4:14) bearing his people the same way.

The gold plate inscribed 'holy to the LORD' (vv 36-38) lets Aaron 'bear the iniquity of the holy things' — the priest absorbs imperfection in worship. Christ bears not flawed worship but the worshippers themselves.
